name: HP Inc.

profile.description: HP Inc. provides personal computing, printing, 3D printing, and hybrid work technologies globally. Operating through Personal Systems ($10.3B Q1 FY2026 revenue) and Printing ($4.2B) segments, the company sells commercial and consumer PCs, notebooks, workstations, printers, supplies, and solutions. Formerly Hewlett-Packard Company, HP was split into HP Inc. (hardware) and Hewlett Packard Enterprise (enterprise IT) in 2015. Founded in 1939, headquartered in Palo Alto, California.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (1/3): HP has executed an AI PC refresh cycle with Personal Systems revenue growing 11% YoY in Q1 FY2026, and has expanded into subscription services (HP All-In Plan), but these are incremental extensions of existing PC/printer hardware lines rather than transformative new products or pivots [1]. No new category creation comparable to a business model transformation in the last 5 years. AI Deployment (1/2): HP is shipping AI-ready PCs with integrated NPUs and has deployed AI tools internally to drive the planned 4,000-6,000 workforce reduction by FY2028, but no quantified AI-specific revenue gains or cost savings have been reported [2]. Market Share (1/2): HP is the #2 global PC maker behind Lenovo with meaningful market presence, but is not gaining share or defining a new category — it participates in a commodity market with thin differentiation [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): AI PC refresh cycle provides near-term visibility with management citing enterprise upgrade demand, but no next-generation products with specific launch dates beyond incremental hardware refreshes [1]. R&D Intensity (0/1): HP's R&D spending (~$1.5B on ~$55B revenue = ~2.7%) is low relative to technology sector peers and reflects the company's hardware-centric, cost-focused model. Total: 1+1+1+1+0 = 4.

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (1/4): HP's purpose — to create technology that makes life better for everyone, everywhere — is generic and interchangeable with virtually any technology company's mission statement [3]. It lacks the specificity and depth that would distinguish HP from any other hardware maker. Community Impact (1/2): HP has invested in sustainability initiatives for two decades, committed to halving GHG emissions by 2030 and net-zero by 2040, and set 75% circular materials targets for products and packaging [4]. These are substantive but industry-standard for a large tech company. Employee Retention (0/2): Glassdoor reviews describe a 'toxic culture and lack of leadership' with employees reporting no innovation, inclusion, or growth mindset [2]. The planned AI-driven workforce reduction of 4,000-6,000 employees (10% of workforce) by FY2028 signals a low-trust environment where employees are explicitly being replaced by technology. Insider Buying (1/2): Neutral — insider transactions show stock awards and some selling by directors and officers, but no meaningful net insider buying. Director Charles Bergh exercised options but no open-market purchases identified [5]. Total: 1+1+0+1 = 3.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. Q1 FY2026 revenue of $14.4B grew 6.9% YoY driven by Personal Systems strength (+11%), though Printing declined 2% [1]. Forward P/E of 6.98 is exceptionally low, reflecting deep value territory and generating a perfect score. Dividend metrics are strong: 61 consecutive years of dividends paid (since 1965), approximately 14 consecutive years of growth post-2015 split, 5yr dividend growth CAGR of 7.6%, and payout ratio of 44.25% [5]. D/E scores 0 due to negative stockholders' equity of -$346M from aggressive share buyback programs that have reduced the equity base below zero. ROE is undefined with negative equity and scores 0. Profit margin of 4.46% is thin for a technology company, reflecting hardware commoditization. Revenue 5yr CAGR is approximately -4.2% (from $62.9B in FY2022 to $55.3B in FY2025) reflecting post-pandemic PC demand normalization. Free cash flow of $2.8B provides solid capital return capacity. Weighted sum: 8.10/12 = 7.

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (4/9): HP faces meaningful AI exposure across its business. The Printing segment ($4.2B/quarter) is under secular decline as AI-driven digital workflows reduce physical printing demand — printing revenue fell 2% YoY even as PCs grew 11% [1]. The PC business faces commoditization pressure as AI shifts value from hardware to software/services, and AI PCs require significant memory cost increases (100% sequential increase in Q1 FY2026) that compress margins [1]. Management's own AI-driven workforce reduction of 4,000-6,000 employees demonstrates that AI directly substitutes for HP's internal labor, and the same dynamic applies to HP's customers' IT purchasing decisions [2]. However, the AI PC refresh cycle provides near-term demand tailwind, and physical hardware manufacturing retains some moat. AI Opportunity (1/1): HP is actively shipping AI-enabled PCs with NPU integration and the AI PC refresh cycle drove 11% Personal Systems growth in Q1 FY2026, representing measurable AI-related hardware revenue [1].

infinite_game.summary: HP under recent leadership exhibits predominantly finite game characteristics. The company has cycled through 6 CEOs in 25 years, with CEO Enrique Lores stepping down in February 2026 and replaced by interim CEO Bruce Broussard, reflecting chronic leadership instability. The AI-driven workforce reduction of 10% explicitly treats employees as cost centers to be optimized, not as contributors to a lasting purpose. HP's strategic decisions are driven by quarterly cost optimization and shareholder returns rather than a vision of enduring impact.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): HP's original 'HP Way' — Bill Hewlett and Dave Packard's commitment to technical contribution and respect for the individual — was a genuine Just Cause validated by decades of execution and cultural influence across Silicon Valley. However, the HP Way has been systematically dismantled through serial CEO transitions, the 2015 company split, recurring layoff cycles, and cost-optimization-driven leadership [2]. The current generic mission ('technology that makes life better') is marketing, not a cause. Sinek would cite HP as a cautionary tale of a company that lost its Just Cause.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (0/2): HP's leadership optimizes for quarterly EPS through share buybacks, cost cuts, and workforce reductions rather than investing in long-term capabilities [1][2]. The decision to reduce headcount by 4,000-6,000 employees via AI replacement is the opposite of courage — it prioritizes near-term cost savings over building organizational capability. Six CEO transitions in 25 years, with the most recent departure in February 2026, reflect a board focused on short-term performance rather than long-term vision.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): Glassdoor reviews explicitly describe 'toxic culture and lack of leadership' with employees reporting that HP is 'a shell of its former legacy' driven by 'short-term cost cutting and site closures' [2]. The announced AI-driven reduction of 4,000-6,000 employees (10% of workforce) by FY2028 explicitly signals that employees are viewed as replaceable cost items. This is the antithesis of Sinek's Circle of Safety — people cannot feel safe when their employer publicly plans to replace them with AI.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): HP has evolved its business model incrementally — expanding from hardware into subscription services (HP All-In Plan), growing 3D printing, and pivoting toward AI-enabled PCs [1]. The 2015 split from Hewlett Packard Enterprise was a meaningful structural change. However, HP remains fundamentally a PC and printer company 87 years after founding — the core business model has not been reinvented. The shifts are incremental adaptations, not transformative pivots driven by a Just Cause.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): HP's competitive framing is finite — focused on market share battles with Lenovo and Dell in PCs, and competitive positioning against Epson and Canon in printing [1]. No evidence in management commentary of acknowledging competitors' strengths as motivation for self-improvement. The competitive language centers on winning share rather than learning and improving.

investment_thesis: HP Inc. offers compelling income value at 6.98x forward earnings with a 5.78% dividend yield backed by 61 consecutive years of payments, 14 years of consecutive growth, and a 7.6% 5-year dividend growth CAGR [1][5]. Q1 FY2026 delivered $14.4B in revenue (+6.9% YoY) with Personal Systems growing 11% on AI PC demand, and management guides FY2026 non-GAAP EPS of $2.90-$3.20 with $2.8-3.0B in free cash flow. The AI PC refresh cycle provides a multi-year demand catalyst as enterprises upgrade aging fleets, and the 44% payout ratio leaves room for continued dividend growth. At $19B market cap, HP trades at less than 7x FCF, creating a significant margin of safety for income investors.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on secular decline in core markets and chronic leadership instability. Printing revenue fell 2% YoY and faces accelerating structural headwinds from digital workflows, while PC margins are being compressed by 100% sequential memory cost increases that management expects to persist [1]. The February 2026 CEO departure — the 6th leadership transition in 25 years — creates strategic uncertainty under interim CEO Bruce Broussard, and Glassdoor reviews describe a 'toxic culture' that suggests organizational dysfunction beyond any single leader [2]. Revenue has contracted from $62.9B (FY2022) to $55.3B (FY2025), a -4.2% CAGR that reflects post-pandemic normalization and underlying demand weakness. The company's negative stockholders' equity (-$346M) from aggressive buybacks means the balance sheet has no equity cushion against cyclical downturns, and the $10.9B debt load requires sustained free cash flow to service.
key_catalysts: Enterprise AI PC refresh cycle driving sustained double-digit Personal Systems growth as organizations upgrade aging fleets with NPU-enabled hardware for on-device AI workloads
Permanent CEO appointment providing strategic clarity and potentially restoring organizational confidence after 6 leadership transitions in 25 years
Subscription services expansion (HP All-In Plan) creating recurring revenue streams that could improve the margin profile beyond commodity hardware economics
key_risks: Secular printing decline accelerating as AI-powered digital workflows reduce physical printing demand, threatening the higher-margin Printing segment that generates disproportionate profit contribution
Memory cost escalation — 100% sequential increase in Q1 FY2026 with further increases expected — compresses PC margins and may force HP to choose between profitability and market share

Leadership vacuum with interim CEO and toxic organizational culture (Glassdoor reviews) creating execution risk during a critical AI PC transition period, compounded by the morale impact of announced 4,000-6,000 AI-driven layoffs
sources: HP Inc. Q1 FY2026 Earnings Release and Guidance — hp.com Newsroom, February 24, 2026
